facts: World War I began in 1914 when a system of European alliances turned a local conflict between Austria-Hungary and Serbia into a continent-wide war, because allied nations were bound by treaties to defend each other.
The United States entered WWI in 1917, not at the start in 1914, because German unrestricted submarine warfare sank American ships and the Zimmerman Telegram revealed a German plot against the US.
Trench warfare on the Western Front caused enormous casualties for minimal territorial gains, because defending troops with machine guns made any frontal attack extremely deadly.
New weapons like poison gas and machine guns led to mass casualties in WWI, because they could kill or injure large numbers of soldiers far more efficiently than in previous conflicts.
The Treaty of Versailles imposed harsh terms on Germany, including heavy reparations and land losses, which created deep resentment that later contributed to the rise of World War II.
Russia's 1917 revolution overthrew the tsar and eventually created the Soviet Union, because severe war losses and economic hardship sparked popular uprisings that the Bolsheviks exploited.
All sides used propaganda in WWI to rally public support and demonize the enemy, by distributing posters, films, and articles that portrayed the war as a just cause.
misconceptions: Students often believe the United States fought in WWI from the very beginning in 1914, but the US actually remained neutral until 1917.
Students think trench warfare was an effective way to quickly win battles, but it actually produced long stalemates and massive casualties with little change in territory.
Students assume the Treaty of Versailles was a fair settlement for all nations, but it placed harsh penalties solely on Germany, which fueled anger and later conflict.
